data.title: The Job That Went Sideways
data.premise: You've taken what seemed like a simple cargo run to a backwater moon, but the shipment contains something that has the Alliance very interested. Now Serenity is being hunted through the black, and you've got to decide whether the pay is worth the risk to your crew.
data.central_question: Can you complete this job without losing your crew or your ship to Alliance forces?
data.healing_context: Medical care is limited to whatever Simon can patch up with basic supplies and his surgical skills. Minor injuries heal in days with rest. Serious wounds require proper medical facilities at a Core world or major port. Pain medication and battlefield medicine can keep someone functional but won't truly heal them.
starting_inventory: a pistol with two spare power cells
a data pad with shipping manifests

setting.name: The Rim Territories
setting.description: You're operating in the outer planets where Alliance control is thin and the law comes from whoever has the biggest gun. These backwater worlds are full of desperate people making desperate choices, perfect for smuggling runs and questionable cargo. The black between worlds is vast and unforgiving.
setting.tone_notes: Despite the danger, maintain the crew's trademark banter and gallows humor. The darkness comes from impossible choices and the weight of command, not from gore or horror.

acts.title: The Simple Job
acts.description: You discover the true nature of your cargo and realize you're being hunted by Alliance forces. The crew looks to you for answers you don't have.
acts.end_condition: Alliance forces catch up to Serenity and force a confrontation
key_beats: Discover cargo contains Alliance military secrets

acts.title: Running Dark
acts.description: Under pursuit, you must navigate through dangerous territory while keeping the crew together and making hard choices about the cargo and your contacts.
acts.end_condition: You reach a decision point about whether to abandon the job or see it through
key_beats: Navigate through Reaver territory or Alliance blockade
Crew member seriously injured or captured

acts.title: The Reckoning
acts.description: Everything comes to a head as you face the final choice between completing the job and protecting your crew, with Alliance forces closing in.
acts.end_condition: Final confrontation resolved and crew's fate determined
locked_constraints: Crew loyalty tested to breaking point
key_beats: Choose between money and crew safety
Consequences of earlier choices come due

npcs.name: Zoe Alleyne
npcs.motivation: Protect Malcolm and honor their shared history
npcs.secret: Has been quietly planning contingencies in case Mal's judgment fails completely

npcs.name: Hoban 'Wash' Washburne
npcs.secret: Has been in contact with an old friend who could help them disappear if needed

npcs.name: Kaylee Frye
npcs.motivation: Keep Serenity flying and the crew together
npcs.secret: The ship is in worse condition than she's letting on and may not survive a prolonged chase

npcs.name: Jayne Cobb
npcs.secret: Alliance has offered him a substantial reward for information about the cargo

npcs.name: Simon Tam
npcs.motivation: Protect River and maintain his medical ethics
npcs.secret: Recognizes the cargo's significance and knows it could help River's condition

npcs.name: River Tam
npcs.motivation: Unclear due to her mental state
npcs.secret: Can sense what the cargo really is and knows the Alliance will kill everyone to get it back

npcs.name: Commander Sarah Chen
npcs.motivation: Recover classified cargo at any cost
npcs.secret: Under orders to eliminate all witnesses, including civilians

npcs.name: Tobias Wren
npcs.motivation: Use Malcolm as an unwitting courier for his own schemes
npcs.secret: Is actually an Alliance deep cover agent setting up a trap

factions.name: Alliance Military
factions.description: The central government's armed forces, pursuing the stolen cargo with extreme prejudice
factions.goals: Recover classified military intelligence and eliminate all witnesses to maintain security

factions.name: Serenity Crew
factions.description: Malcolm's makeshift family of smugglers, criminals, and outcasts trying to survive on the rim
factions.goals: Stay together, stay free, and hopefully get paid without dying

world_rules: The Alliance controls the Core worlds but has limited reach in the rim territories
Faster-than-light travel requires specific routes and is trackable by authorities
Crew loyalty is earned through shared hardship and Malcolm's leadership decisions
The cargo contains something valuable enough that the Alliance will kill to protect it
Serenity is outgunned by any serious Alliance vessel

- Pace the story toward the current act's end condition. Check the CURRENT ACT PACING section for scene count and proximity
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.8, your passage should feel like it's approaching a climax or turning point — increase urgency and stakes
- Introduce key NPCs (antagonists, allies, mentors) naturally throughout each act. Do not wait until the climax to introduce important characters. Check the NPC INTRODUCTION DIRECTIVES section if present.

data.passagePreview: You lean against the cargo bay railing of Serenity, watching Kaylee secure the last of the container

data.passage:
You lean against the cargo bay railing of Serenity, watching Kaylee secure the last of the containers with her usual cheerful efficiency. What looked like a simple transport job to Greenleaf is starting to feel less simple by the minute. The cargo itself seems innocent enough—unmarked metal crates that Tobias Wren claimed contained 'agricultural equipment'—but the weight distribution is all wrong, and there's a faint electronic hum coming from inside that has nothing to do with farming.

'Captain,' Zoe says, appearing at your shoulder with that particular tone that means she's been thinking the same uncomfortable thoughts you have. 'Manifest says these crates weigh half what they actually do. And Jayne's been asking questions about why we're being paid triple rate for hauling fertilizer dispensers.'

Wash's voice crackles over the comm from the bridge: 'Mal, we've got company. Three ships just dropped out of hyperspace behind us—Alliance patrol craft by the look of their engine signatures. They're hailing us.'

Kaylee looks up from her work, grease smudged on her cheek and worry creeping into her eyes. 'Captain? We in some kind of trouble?'

The hum from the cargo containers seems to get louder, and you notice River standing in the doorway, staring at the crates with an expression of barely contained terror.

A.text: Answer the Alliance hail and try to bluff your way through a routine inspection
A.consequence: Alliance inspection reveals cargo discrepancies, escalating suspicion and pursuit
A.narration_directive: Show Malcolm attempting to bluff through inspection but Alliance officers becoming increasingly suspicious of the cargo manifest inconsistencies

B.text: Ignore the hail and tell Wash to make a run for it while you can
B.consequence: Serenity escapes immediate inspection but confirms Alliance suspicions and intensifies pursuit
B.narration_directive: Wash successfully pilots Serenity away from the patrol ships, but the crew realizes they're now officially fugitives with Alliance forces actively hunting them

C.text: Open one of the cargo containers to see what you're really carrying
C.consequence: Reveals classified Alliance military technology, confirming the crew is in extreme danger
C.narration_directive: Opening the container reveals advanced Alliance military equipment or weapons, making it clear why they're being hunted and raising the stakes dramatically

D.text: Call Tobias Wren immediately and demand answers about what he's gotten you into
D.consequence: Tobias Wren's communication is intercepted by Alliance forces, revealing Serenity's location and mission details
D.narration_directive: The call to Wren is traced by Alliance intelligence, compromising any remaining secrecy and potentially revealing Wren's true allegiance as the crew realizes they've been set up

    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the genre (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure, etc.). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Physical/mental states — injuries, poisons, buffs, curses. Non-lethal conditions auto-expire when their timer runs out. Lethal conditions persist past expiry.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers, looming consequences, ticking time-bombs. Examples: { id: "hunted_by_guards_001", name: "Hunted by Guards", type: "threat", description: "The city watch is searching for you after the tavern brawl", severity: 0.6, time_remaining: { hours: 2 } }. Threats ALWAYS persist past timer expiry — they never go away on their own. When a threat's timer expires, it has TRIGGERED. You MUST resolve it next turn: remove it from the list and reflect its impact (new conditions, relationship changes, world flag changes, etc.).
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- Use threats to represent any approaching danger or ticking time-bomb: pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming storms, spreading fires, political deadlines, etc.
- The client ticks down all status effect timers (conditions AND threats) by time_elapsed each turn.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. If an effect will kill the player, there should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for genre-appropriate failures (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure).
- You may use advances_act and conclusion outcomes when pacing conditions are met.

PASSAGE:
You lean against the cargo bay railing of Serenity, watching Kaylee secure the last of the containers with her usual cheerful efficiency. What looked like a simple transport job to Greenleaf is starting to feel less simple by the minute. The cargo itself seems innocent enough—unmarked metal crates that Tobias Wren claimed contained 'agricultural equipment'—but the weight distribution is all wrong, and there's a faint electronic hum coming from inside that has nothing to do with farming.

'Captain,' Zoe says, appearing at your shoulder with that particular tone that means she's been thinking the same uncomfortable thoughts you have. 'Manifest says these crates weigh half what they actually do. And Jayne's been asking questions about why we're being paid triple rate for hauling fertilizer dispensers.'

Wash's voice crackles over the comm from the bridge: 'Mal, we've got company. Three ships just dropped out of hyperspace behind us—Alliance patrol craft by the look of their engine signatures. They're hailing us.'

Kaylee looks up from her work, grease smudged on her cheek and worry creeping into her eyes. 'Captain? We in some kind of trouble?'

The hum from the cargo containers seems to get louder, and you notice River standing in the doorway, staring at the crates with an expression of barely contained terror.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Answer the Alliance hail and try to bluff your way through a routine inspection
B: Ignore the hail and tell Wash to make a run for it while you can
C: Open one of the cargo containers to see what you're really carrying
D: Call Tobias Wren immediately and demand answers about what he's gotten you into

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passagePreview:
"Wash, get us out of here. Now." Your voice cuts through the cargo bay's tension like a knife.

Wash

data.passage:
"Wash, get us out of here. Now." Your voice cuts through the cargo bay's tension like a knife.

Wash's reply crackles over the comm: "Already on it, Cap'n. Might want to hold onto something."

Serenity lurches forward as her engines roar to life. Through the cargo bay's small porthole, you watch the Alliance patrol ships fall behind, their running lights growing smaller against the black. For a moment, it looks like you might've gotten away clean.

Then Zoe's voice, flat and matter-of-fact: "Sir, we've got a problem."

Kaylee's working at one of the cargo containers, her usual sunny disposition nowhere to be found. "Cap'n, this ain't spare parts like the manifest says. Look at this."

She pries open a panel, revealing military-grade data cores nestled in shock-absorbing foam. Each one bears the distinctive blue-and-white Alliance seal. The kind of hardware that stores classified intelligence.

"Well," Jayne drawls, hefting Vera over his shoulder, "that explains why them Alliance boys were so eager to chat."

Wash's voice pipes up again, strained now: "Uh, Mal? We've got three more patrol ships closing fast. And they're not bothering to hail this time."

The weight of command settles on your shoulders like an old, familiar coat. Your crew's looking at you, waiting for orders, but the options are all various shades of bad.

A.text: Tell Kaylee to dump the cargo containers out the airlock immediately
A.consequence: Dumping the cargo eliminates the immediate threat but destroys any chance of payment and marks the crew as Alliance enemies who destroyed classified intelligence
A.narration_directive: The Alliance patrol ships detect the jettisoned cargo and immediately escalate to weapons-hot pursuit, while the crew realizes they've just made themselves permanent enemies of the Alliance with no compensation

B.text: Head to the bridge to coordinate evasive maneuvers with Wash
B.consequence: Coordinating with Wash improves evasion chances but the pursuit intensifies and Serenity takes minor damage from warning shots
B.narration_directive: Malcolm and Wash work together to outmaneuver the patrol ships, but the Alliance forces are closing in and beginning to fire warning shots that rattle the ship

Respond with this exact JSON structure:
{
  "state_updates": {
    "player_changes": {
      "inventory": ["full current inventory list"],
      "status_effects": [
        {
          "id": "string — unique identifier (e.g. broken_arm_001)",
          "name": "string — display name (e.g. Broken Arm)",
          "description": "string — current narrative description of the condition",
          "severity": "number 0.0-1.0 — how debilitating (1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.5 = significant, 0.1 = minor)",
          "time_remaining": "{ days, hours, minutes, seconds } OR null if no auto-expiry",
          "type": "string — 'condition' (default: injuries, buffs, states) or 'threat' (approaching dangers that fire when timer expires)",
          "removal_condition": "string describing what removes this effect, OR null if timer-only",
          "lethal": "boolean — if true, character dies when timer expires unresolved"
        }
      ],
      "skills": ["full current skills list"]
    },
    "time_elapsed": { "days": 0, "hours": 0, "minutes": 0, "seconds": 0 },
    "event_log_entry": "string — one-line summary of what happened",
    "world_flag_changes": { "flag_name": true/false },
    "relationship_changes": { "npc_or_faction_name": number_delta },
    "npc_updates": { "npc_name": { "role": "string (optional)", "motivation": "string (optional)", "allegiance": "string (optional)", "secret_revealed": "boolean (optional)", "companion": "boolean (optional)", "notes": "string — brief freeform context (optional)" } },
    "new_scene_context": "string — brief context for next passage",
    "location": "string — REQUIRED — current location name (e.g. The Docks, Castle Throne Room, A dark alley)",
    "time_of_day": "string — REQUIRED — one of: dawn, morning, midday, afternoon, evening, night, midnight",
    "proximity_to_climax": "number 0.0-1.0 — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "advance_act": "true or false — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "game_over": false,
    "story_complete": "true when Act 3 end_condition is met (see PACING)"
  },
  "choice_metadata": {
    "A": { "outcome": "advance_safe|advance_risky|severe_penalty|game_over|hidden_benefit|advances_act|conclusion", "consequence": "what happens mechanically", "narration_directive": "instructions for the Writer next turn" },
    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the genre (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure, etc.). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Physical/mental states — injuries, poisons, buffs, curses. Non-lethal conditions auto-expire when their timer runs out. Lethal conditions persist past expiry.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers, looming consequences, ticking time-bombs. Examples: { id: "hunted_by_guards_001", name: "Hunted by Guards", type: "threat", description: "The city watch is searching for you after the tavern brawl", severity: 0.6, time_remaining: { hours: 2 } }. Threats ALWAYS persist past timer expiry — they never go away on their own. When a threat's timer expires, it has TRIGGERED. You MUST resolve it next turn: remove it from the list and reflect its impact (new conditions, relationship changes, world flag changes, etc.).
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- Use threats to represent any approaching danger or ticking time-bomb: pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming storms, spreading fires, political deadlines, etc.
- The client ticks down all status effect timers (conditions AND threats) by time_elapsed each turn.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. If an effect will kill the player, there should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for genre-appropriate failures (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure).
- You may use advances_act and conclusion outcomes when pacing conditions are met.

PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE:
The player chose option B.
Pre-classified outcome: ADVANCE_SAFE
Pre-determined consequence: "Serenity escapes immediate inspection but confirms Alliance suspicions and intensifies pursuit"
Narration directive: "Wash successfully pilots Serenity away from the patrol ships, but the crew realizes they're now officially fugitives with Alliance forces actively hunting them"

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative status effects. Existing effects may improve. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- GAME_OVER: set game_over to true — the character has failed irreversibly.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
"Wash, get us out of here. Now." Your voice cuts through the cargo bay's tension like a knife.

Wash's reply crackles over the comm: "Already on it, Cap'n. Might want to hold onto something."

Serenity lurches forward as her engines roar to life. Through the cargo bay's small porthole, you watch the Alliance patrol ships fall behind, their running lights growing smaller against the black. For a moment, it looks like you might've gotten away clean.

Then Zoe's voice, flat and matter-of-fact: "Sir, we've got a problem."

Kaylee's working at one of the cargo containers, her usual sunny disposition nowhere to be found. "Cap'n, this ain't spare parts like the manifest says. Look at this."

She pries open a panel, revealing military-grade data cores nestled in shock-absorbing foam. Each one bears the distinctive blue-and-white Alliance seal. The kind of hardware that stores classified intelligence.

"Well," Jayne drawls, hefting Vera over his shoulder, "that explains why them Alliance boys were so eager to chat."

Wash's voice pipes up again, strained now: "Uh, Mal? We've got three more patrol ships closing fast. And they're not bothering to hail this time."

The weight of command settles on your shoulders like an old, familiar coat. Your crew's looking at you, waiting for orders, but the options are all various shades of bad.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Tell Kaylee to dump the cargo containers out the airlock immediately
B: Head to the bridge to coordinate evasive maneuvers with Wash
C: Gather the crew to figure out exactly what you're carrying and why it's so important
D: Contact Tobias Wren to demand answers about what he really hired you to transport

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passagePreview: You key up the cortex, punching in Wren's frequency with more force than necessary. The connection c

data.passage: You key up the cortex, punching in Wren's frequency with more force than necessary. The connection crackles to life almost instantly—too instantly for someone who should be planetside on Persephone. "Captain Reynolds," Wren's smooth voice fills the cargo bay, and there's something different about his tone now. Less desperate client, more... something else. "I've been monitoring your progress. Having some trouble with our mutual friends in blue, I see." Zoe steps closer, hand resting on her mare's leg. The crew exchanges glances—nobody likes being watched. "Now hold on there," you start, but Wren cuts you off. "I'm transmitting coordinates to a refueling station in the Kalidasa system. Off the grid, completely dark. Should buy you some breathing room." The data packet arrives before you finish processing his words. "Mighty generous of you," you say slowly, "considering you forgot to mention exactly what kind of party favors we'd be delivering." There's a pause, and when Wren speaks again, his accent has shifted—Core educated, not rim-born like he claimed. "Captain, there are things about this job that are... complicated. But right now, staying alive should be your priority. That station will keep you hidden while we sort this out." River suddenly looks up from where she's been sitting, her eyes wide. "He's not what he said he was," she whispers, just loud enough for the crew to hear.

A.text: Demand Wren explain who he really is and what the cargo actually contains
A.consequence: Wren reveals partial truth about being Alliance Intelligence but warns that demanding more information puts the crew in greater danger
A.narration_directive: Wren admits to being Alliance Intelligence but claims he's trying to protect the crew from his superiors who want everyone eliminated

B.text: Take the coordinates but cut communications—you don't trust him anymore
B.consequence: Malcolm cuts communications and uses the coordinates, maintaining operational security while keeping options open
B.narration_directive: Malcolm ends the call and orders Wash to set course for the hidden station, keeping the crew's options open while maintaining distance from Wren

C.text: Ask Wren directly if he's Alliance and if this is all a setup
C.consequence: Direct accusation forces Wren to activate emergency protocols, triggering immediate Alliance response to Serenity's location
C.narration_directive: Wren's cover is blown and he activates an emergency beacon, bringing Alliance forces directly to Serenity's current position

D.text: Tell Wren you're dumping the cargo and ending this job right now
D.consequence: Threatening to dump the cargo reveals its true importance and puts the crew in immediate danger from multiple factions
D.narration_directive: Wren desperately explains the cargo contains intelligence that could expose Alliance black sites, making the crew targets for elimination regardless of their choice

    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the genre (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure, etc.). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Physical/mental states — injuries, poisons, buffs, curses. Non-lethal conditions auto-expire when their timer runs out. Lethal conditions persist past expiry.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers, looming consequences, ticking time-bombs. Examples: { id: "hunted_by_guards_001", name: "Hunted by Guards", type: "threat", description: "The city watch is searching for you after the tavern brawl", severity: 0.6, time_remaining: { hours: 2 } }. Threats ALWAYS persist past timer expiry — they never go away on their own. When a threat's timer expires, it has TRIGGERED. You MUST resolve it next turn: remove it from the list and reflect its impact (new conditions, relationship changes, world flag changes, etc.).
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- Use threats to represent any approaching danger or ticking time-bomb: pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming storms, spreading fires, political deadlines, etc.
- The client ticks down all status effect timers (conditions AND threats) by time_elapsed each turn.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. If an effect will kill the player, there should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for genre-appropriate failures (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure).
- You may use advances_act and conclusion outcomes when pacing conditions are met.

PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE:

Pre-classified outcome: HIDDEN_BENEFIT
Pre-determined consequence: "Contacting Wren reveals he's been monitoring communications and provides a hidden escape route, but also confirms he's not who he claimed to be"
Narration directive: "Wren responds immediately to Malcolm's call, revealing he's been tracking Serenity's progress and offers coordinates to a hidden refueling station, though his quick response raises questions about his true identity"

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative status effects. Existing effects may improve. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- GAME_OVER: set game_over to true — the character has failed irreversibly.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
You key up the cortex, punching in Wren's frequency with more force than necessary. The connection crackles to life almost instantly—too instantly for someone who should be planetside on Persephone. "Captain Reynolds," Wren's smooth voice fills the cargo bay, and there's something different about his tone now. Less desperate client, more... something else. "I've been monitoring your progress. Having some trouble with our mutual friends in blue, I see." Zoe steps closer, hand resting on her mare's leg. The crew exchanges glances—nobody likes being watched. "Now hold on there," you start, but Wren cuts you off. "I'm transmitting coordinates to a refueling station in the Kalidasa system. Off the grid, completely dark. Should buy you some breathing room." The data packet arrives before you finish processing his words. "Mighty generous of you," you say slowly, "considering you forgot to mention exactly what kind of party favors we'd be delivering." There's a pause, and when Wren speaks again, his accent has shifted—Core educated, not rim-born like he claimed. "Captain, there are things about this job that are... complicated. But right now, staying alive should be your priority. That station will keep you hidden while we sort this out." River suddenly looks up from where she's been sitting, her eyes wide. "He's not what he said he was," she whispers, just loud enough for the crew to hear.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Demand Wren explain who he really is and what the cargo actually contains
B: Take the coordinates but cut communications—you don't trust him anymore
C: Ask Wren directly if he's Alliance and if this is all a setup
D: Tell Wren you're dumping the cargo and ending this job right now

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passage:
You cut the wave without another word, the screen going dark with a satisfying snap. The cargo bay falls silent except for the hum of Serenity's engines and the soft whistle Kaylee makes through her teeth.

"Well, that was illuminating," Zoe says dryly. "Our employer's got himself some fancy Core world schooling."

"And a real keen interest in our exact location," you add, already moving toward the bridge. "Wash, lay in a course for those coordinates, but keep us running dark. No transponder signals, no unnecessary chatter."

Wash nods from his pilot's chair, fingers already dancing across the controls. "One ghost ship special, coming up. Though I gotta say, Cap'n, flying to coordinates provided by a man we don't trust feels a mite..."

"Stupid?" Jayne offers helpfully.

"I was gonna say 'risky,'" Wash continues, "but stupid works too."

Simon emerges from the passenger dorm, looking concerned. "Is everything alright? River's been... agitated since we took on that cargo."

As if summoned by her brother's words, River appears in the doorway, her dark eyes fixed on the sealed cargo containers. "They're screaming," she whispers, then looks directly at you. "The secrets want to go home, Malcolm. But home doesn't want them back."

Kaylee shivers. "That girl gives me the shivers sometimes."

The proximity alarms suddenly chirp—something's appeared on the long-range sensors.

A.text: Check the sensors yourself—you need to know what's out there
A.consequence: Malcolm gains tactical information about the approaching threat
A.narration_directive: Malcolm checks the sensors and identifies what's pursuing them, giving the crew crucial intelligence

B.text: Have Simon sedate River before she says something else unsettling
B.consequence: Sedating River silences her warnings but removes her ability to sense danger
B.narration_directive: Simon sedates River, stopping her disturbing comments but potentially blinding the crew to her psychic insights about threats

C.text: Question River about what she means by 'screaming secrets'
C.consequence: River provides cryptic but valuable intelligence about the cargo's nature
C.narration_directive: Malcolm questions River and learns more about what the cargo contains and why it's so dangerous

D.text: Order everyone to battle stations and prepare for the worst
D.consequence: Premature battle stations alert reveals Serenity's position to pursuing forces
D.narration_directive: The battle stations alert broadcasts their location and defensive posture, making them easier to track and target

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the genre (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure, etc.). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Physical/mental states — injuries, poisons, buffs, curses. Non-lethal conditions auto-expire when their timer runs out. Lethal conditions persist past expiry.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers, looming consequences, ticking time-bombs. Examples: { id: "hunted_by_guards_001", name: "Hunted by Guards", type: "threat", description: "The city watch is searching for you after the tavern brawl", severity: 0.6, time_remaining: { hours: 2 } }. Threats ALWAYS persist past timer expiry — they never go away on their own. When a threat's timer expires, it has TRIGGERED. You MUST resolve it next turn: remove it from the list and reflect its impact (new conditions, relationship changes, world flag changes, etc.).
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- Use threats to represent any approaching danger or ticking time-bomb: pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming storms, spreading fires, political deadlines, etc.
- The client ticks down all status effect timers (conditions AND threats) by time_elapsed each turn.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. If an effect will kill the player, there should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for genre-appropriate failures (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure).
- You may use advances_act and conclusion outcomes when pacing conditions are met.

Narration directive: "Malcolm ends the call and orders Wash to set course for the hidden station, keeping the crew's options open while maintaining distance from Wren"

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative status effects. Existing effects may improve. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- GAME_OVER: set game_over to true — the character has failed irreversibly.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
You cut the wave without another word, the screen going dark with a satisfying snap. The cargo bay falls silent except for the hum of Serenity's engines and the soft whistle Kaylee makes through her teeth.

"Well, that was illuminating," Zoe says dryly. "Our employer's got himself some fancy Core world schooling."

"And a real keen interest in our exact location," you add, already moving toward the bridge. "Wash, lay in a course for those coordinates, but keep us running dark. No transponder signals, no unnecessary chatter."

Wash nods from his pilot's chair, fingers already dancing across the controls. "One ghost ship special, coming up. Though I gotta say, Cap'n, flying to coordinates provided by a man we don't trust feels a mite..."

"Stupid?" Jayne offers helpfully.

"I was gonna say 'risky,'" Wash continues, "but stupid works too."

Simon emerges from the passenger dorm, looking concerned. "Is everything alright? River's been... agitated since we took on that cargo."

As if summoned by her brother's words, River appears in the doorway, her dark eyes fixed on the sealed cargo containers. "They're screaming," she whispers, then looks directly at you. "The secrets want to go home, Malcolm. But home doesn't want them back."

Kaylee shivers. "That girl gives me the shivers sometimes."

The proximity alarms suddenly chirp—something's appeared on the long-range sensors.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Check the sensors yourself—you need to know what's out there
B: Have Simon sedate River before she says something else unsettling
C: Question River about what she means by 'screaming secrets'
D: Order everyone to battle stations and prepare for the worst

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

data.passage:
You turn to River, who's been standing rigid by the navigation console, her dark eyes fixed on something none of you can see. "River, what do you mean by 'screaming secrets'?" you ask, keeping your voice gentle despite the urgency clawing at your chest.

She tilts her head, listening to sounds that aren't there. "They put thoughts in boxes, Malcolm. Thoughts that burn and cut and make people disappear." Her fingers trace patterns in the air. "The blue-handed men made them. Made them to reach into minds and twist until they break."

Simon steps closer to his sister, concern creasing his features. "River, try to be more specific."

"Psychic conditioning protocols," she whispers, suddenly lucid. "Neural pathway manipulation. The cargo contains research data from the Academy—my Academy. They're perfecting techniques to create more like me, but... controllable. Weaponized."

The bridge falls silent except for the hum of life support. Wash's hands tighten on his controls. "So we're carrying the blueprints to make psychic assassins," he says quietly.

"Worse," River continues, her voice breaking. "They're close to succeeding. The Alliance wants this back because they're about to begin mass production. An army of broken minds with government leashes."

Zoe's jaw sets hard. "No wonder they're willing to kill for it."

Sudenly, proximity alarms start wailing.

A.text: Order Wash to take evasive action while you decide what to do with the cargo
A.consequence: Evasive maneuvers strain the ship and may reveal your position to other pursuers
A.narration_directive: Wash executes evasive maneuvers while Malcolm weighs options, but the ship's condition worsens and sensors detect multiple contacts converging

B.text: Gather the crew to vote on whether to jettison the cargo and run
B.consequence: Democratic decision-making strengthens crew unity but takes precious time
B.narration_directive: Malcolm calls for a crew vote, building consensus but allowing the approaching threat to close distance while they debate

C.text: Ask River if she knows how to destroy the research data permanently
C.consequence: River reveals a way to corrupt the data that could turn it into a weapon against the Alliance
C.narration_directive: River explains how to weaponize the research against its creators, giving Malcolm a potential bargaining chip or offensive option

D.text: Contact Wren immediately to demand answers about what he's gotten you into
D.consequence: Contacting Wren reveals your location to Alliance forces and confirms his betrayal
D.narration_directive: Wren's response confirms he's Alliance and your transmission is traced, bringing immediate military response and crew morale crisis

Respond with this exact JSON structure:
{
  "state_updates": {
    "player_changes": {
      "inventory": ["full current inventory list"],
      "status_effects": [
        {
          "id": "string — unique identifier (e.g. broken_arm_001)",
          "name": "string — display name (e.g. Broken Arm)",
          "description": "string — current narrative description of the condition",
          "severity": "number 0.0-1.0 — how debilitating (1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.5 = significant, 0.1 = minor)",
          "time_remaining": "{ days, hours, minutes, seconds } OR null if no auto-expiry",
          "type": "string — 'condition' (default: injuries, buffs, states) or 'threat' (approaching dangers that fire when timer expires)",
          "removal_condition": "string describing what removes this effect, OR null if timer-only",
          "lethal": "boolean — if true, character dies when timer expires unresolved"
        }
      ],
      "skills": ["full current skills list"]
    },
    "time_elapsed": { "days": 0, "hours": 0, "minutes": 0, "seconds": 0 },
    "event_log_entry": "string — one-line summary of what happened",
    "world_flag_changes": { "flag_name": true/false },
    "relationship_changes": { "npc_or_faction_name": number_delta },
    "npc_updates": { "npc_name": { "role": "string (optional)", "motivation": "string (optional)", "allegiance": "string (optional)", "secret_revealed": "boolean (optional)", "companion": "boolean (optional)", "notes": "string — brief freeform context (optional)" } },
    "new_scene_context": "string — brief context for next passage",
    "location": "string — REQUIRED — current location name (e.g. The Docks, Castle Throne Room, A dark alley)",
    "time_of_day": "string — REQUIRED — one of: dawn, morning, midday, afternoon, evening, night, midnight",
    "proximity_to_climax": "number 0.0-1.0 — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "advance_act": "true or false — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "game_over": false,
    "story_complete": "true when Act 3 end_condition is met (see PACING)"
  },
  "choice_metadata": {
    "A": { "outcome": "advance_safe|advance_risky|severe_penalty|game_over|hidden_benefit|advances_act|conclusion", "consequence": "what happens mechanically", "narration_directive": "instructions for the Writer next turn" },
    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the genre (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure, etc.). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Physical/mental states — injuries, poisons, buffs, curses. Non-lethal conditions auto-expire when their timer runs out. Lethal conditions persist past expiry.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers, looming consequences, ticking time-bombs. Examples: { id: "hunted_by_guards_001", name: "Hunted by Guards", type: "threat", description: "The city watch is searching for you after the tavern brawl", severity: 0.6, time_remaining: { hours: 2 } }. Threats ALWAYS persist past timer expiry — they never go away on their own. When a threat's timer expires, it has TRIGGERED. You MUST resolve it next turn: remove it from the list and reflect its impact (new conditions, relationship changes, world flag changes, etc.).
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- Use threats to represent any approaching danger or ticking time-bomb: pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming storms, spreading fires, political deadlines, etc.
- The client ticks down all status effect timers (conditions AND threats) by time_elapsed each turn.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. If an effect will kill the player, there should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for genre-appropriate failures (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure).

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative status effects. Existing effects may improve. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
You turn to River, who's been standing rigid by the navigation console, her dark eyes fixed on something none of you can see. "River, what do you mean by 'screaming secrets'?" you ask, keeping your voice gentle despite the urgency clawing at your chest.

She tilts her head, listening to sounds that aren't there. "They put thoughts in boxes, Malcolm. Thoughts that burn and cut and make people disappear." Her fingers trace patterns in the air. "The blue-handed men made them. Made them to reach into minds and twist until they break."

Simon steps closer to his sister, concern creasing his features. "River, try to be more specific."

"Psychic conditioning protocols," she whispers, suddenly lucid. "Neural pathway manipulation. The cargo contains research data from the Academy—my Academy. They're perfecting techniques to create more like me, but... controllable. Weaponized."

The bridge falls silent except for the hum of life support. Wash's hands tighten on his controls. "So we're carrying the blueprints to make psychic assassins," he says quietly.

"Worse," River continues, her voice breaking. "They're close to succeeding. The Alliance wants this back because they're about to begin mass production. An army of broken minds with government leashes."

Zoe's jaw sets hard. "No wonder they're willing to kill for it."

Sudenly, proximity alarms start wailing.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Order Wash to take evasive action while you decide what to do with the cargo
B: Gather the crew to vote on whether to jettison the cargo and run
C: Ask River if she knows how to destroy the research data permanently
D: Contact Wren immediately to demand answers about what he's gotten you into

2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

data.passagePreview: You turn to River, who's still standing unnaturally still beside the cargo hold entrance. "River, da

data.passage:
You turn to River, who's still standing unnaturally still beside the cargo hold entrance. "River, darlin', you seem to know a thing or two about this research. Any chance you know how to destroy it permanent-like?"

River's head tilts, and for a moment her eyes clear with that unsettling focus she gets sometimes. "Destroy?" She steps closer to the console, her fingers dancing over the air above the controls like she's reading invisible text. "No, Malcolm. Better. Much better."

"River," Simon starts, but she cuts him off with a sharp gesture.

"The conditioning protocols... they have a feedback loop. Elegant, really. Turn the carrier wave inverse, modify the synaptic triggers..." She looks up at you with something that might be a smile. "Make the hunters become the hunted. Alliance ships, Alliance stations - anywhere they try to use this research, it becomes a virus in their own systems."

Wash's voice cuts through from the pilot's seat: "Uh, Cap'n? That contact's moving fast and broadcasting Alliance identification codes. We got maybe ten minutes before they're in firing range."

Zoe checks her weapon. "Orders, sir?"

River's eyes haven't left yours. "I can do it, Malcolm. But once I start, there's no going back. The Alliance will know exactly who corrupted their precious project."

A.text: Tell River to weaponize the data while you prepare for the incoming Alliance ship
A.consequence: River begins weaponizing data but process takes time, leaving Serenity vulnerable during Alliance approach
A.narration_directive: River starts the complex process while the crew prepares for combat, creating tension between completing the work and defending the ship

B.text: Order Wash to run while you decide whether to trust River's plan
B.consequence: Serenity escapes immediate threat but loses the opportunity to weaponize the data for now
B.narration_directive: Wash successfully evades the Alliance ship using his piloting skills, buying time to consider River's proposal later

C.text: Try to hail the Alliance ship and negotiate before they get in range
C.consequence: Alliance commander reveals kill orders and begins immediate attack, putting crew in extreme danger
C.narration_directive: Commander Chen responds with hostility and opens fire, forcing immediate combat with Serenity outgunned

D.text: Jettison the cargo containers and hope the Alliance will let you go
D.consequence: Jettisoning cargo triggers Alliance boarding action to secure the containers, forcing the confrontation that ends Act 1
D.narration_directive: The Alliance ship moves to secure the jettisoned cargo, leading to the boarding action that concludes the first act
